coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of CQDs/Ag NPs composites with photoluminescence and their potential application in antibacterial materials
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Youliang
Wei, Ying
Fang, Changqing
Chen, Jing
Zhao, Weina
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Ag NPs have been applied in many fields due to excellent optical performances and other special characteristics. However, how to release their superimposed effects is still a challenge. For utilizing the properties of CQDs and Ag NPs synergistically, CQDs/Ag NPs composites were synthesized via in-situ method assisted by ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation. The diameter of as-prepared CQDs/Ag NPs composites was in the range of 20–28 nm, and the absorption band of visible light by CQDs/Ag NPs-6 was expanded to 450 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission peak for CQDs /Ag NPs appeared at 393 nm. The antibacterial properties of CQDs/Ag NPs composites were better than silver nitrate and solo Ag NPs solutions. Therefore, this paper can provide a facile and favorable method for preparing excellent antibacterial materials in future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Silver nanoparticles
Photoluminescence
Antibacterial property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs via hydrothermal method
Preparation of Ag NPs under UV light irradiation
Synthesis of CQDs/Ag NPs composites via in-situ method
Components and morphologies of CQDs and CQDs/Ag NPs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The full-range XPS of the CQDs/Ag NPs composites, (b) XPS spectrum in the Ag 3d region.

(a) FTIR spectrum and sample photograph of CQDs, (b) TEM image of CQDs, (c) TEM image of CQDs/Ag NPs-1, (d) TEM image of CQDs/Ag NPs-2, (e) TEM image of CQDs/Ag NPs-3, (f) TEM image of CQDs/Ag NPs-6, (g-4) mapping image of CQDs/Ag NPs-6.
(a) Photographs of CQDs/Ag NPs, (b) synthesis mechanism diagram for preparing CQDs/Ag NPs by in-situ reduction under UV irradiation.
(a) UV–vis spectra of CQDs/Ag NPs obtained under different UV irradiation time, (b) UV–vis spectra of CQDs/Ag NPs and Ag NPs (from Ag NPs samples prepared in section 2.3).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs, CQDs/Ag NPs-2, CQDs/Ag NPs-3 and CQDs/Ag NPs-6; (b) absorption spectra of as-prepared Ag NPs-2 (from Ag NPs samples prepared in section 2.3) and fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs.

Representative images of agar plates containing CQDs, silver nitrate, Ag NPs-1, Ag NPs-2, CQDs/Ag NPs-3 and CQDs/Ag NPs-6 impregnated disks and diameter of inhibition zone for E. coli (a) and S. aureus (b).
The diameter of inhibition zone for E. coli and S. aureus on CQDs, silver nitrate, Ag NPs-1, Ag NPs-2, CQDs/Ag NPs-3 and CQDs/Ag NPs-6.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of CQDs/Ag NPs composites with photoluminescence and their potential application in antibacterial materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital Media Technology, Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an 710048, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital Media Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital Media Technology, Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an 710048, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Ag NPs have been applied in many fields due to excellent optical performances and other special characteristics. However, how to release their superimposed effects is still a challenge. For utilizing the properties of CQDs and Ag NPs synergistically, CQDs/Ag NPs composites were synthesized via in-situ method assisted by ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation. The diameter of as-prepared CQDs/Ag NPs composites was in the range of 20–28 nm, and the absorption band of visible light by CQDs/Ag NPs-6 was expanded to 450 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission peak for CQDs /Ag NPs appeared at 393 nm. The antibacterial properties of CQDs/Ag NPs composites were better than silver nitrate and solo Ag NPs solutions. Therefore, this paper can provide a facile and favorable method for preparing excellent antibacterial materials in future.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with some fascinating properties, which consist of carbon nanoparticles of less than 10 nm in size [1]. The discovery of CQDs in 2004 triggered subsequent studies to explore their properties [2]. CQDs gained the high quantum yield by optimizing the synthesis process, and the luminescence properties can be regulated by changing the size [3-5]. The fluorescence of CQDs could be effectively quenched by electron acceptor or electron donor in solution [6]. The routes for synthesizing CQDs mainly include top-down and bottom-up methods. Based on the latter, CQDs were prepared via combustion template method, hydrothermal/solvothermal method, or microwave method [7]. Owing to the excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, high water-solubility, low cytotoxicity, and environmental friendliness, CQDs have been widely applied in chemical sensing, biosensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [3]. Therefore, CQDs were compounded with metals [8] and semiconductor oxides [9-13] to explore their applications by many researchers. Sheng and co-workers combined CQDs with ZnO and used them as optoelectronic devices [9]. As-prepared composites including of Fe2O3/CQDs and ZnO/CQDs can degrade gas phase benzene effectively [10,11]. The composites of CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/Cu2O exhibited excellent photocatalytic performances [12,13]. In addition, CQDs have emerged as an effective supporting material for the synthesis of Ag NPs by a simple and fast way [14-16].

For CQDs/Ag NPs composites, Zhuo and co-workers successfully used CQDs and the reductants to prepare Ag@CQDs NPs and Au@CQDs NPs with nucleus-shell structures [15]. After modifying the surface of Ag NPs, Ag@CQDs NPs also were used as surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate and nano-catalysts [16]. During the synthesis process, the reductants and/or surface modification were essential, which may cause uncertain factors. In addition, it is necessary to add the surfactant to prevent the agglomeration of Ag NPs. Some researchers used the checkerboard test method to combine the two antibacterial agents of CQDs and Ag NPs, and they acted on Staphylococcus aureus. These antibacterial agents had significant synergistic effects on Staphylococcus aureus [17]. Hu and co-workers introduced polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) into CQDs and utilized them to synthesize Ag NPs [18]. Compared with pure PVA hydrogels, PVA/CQDs-Ag hydrogels can greatly improve the antibacterial activity of PVA/CQDs hydrogels. Ag-based nanomaterials have been widely used in antibacterial field, however, the bioavailability will be low if the materials have poor stability. Therefore, controlling the stability and improving the antibacterial properties of Ag-based nanomaterials is the key to construct excellent antibacterial materials [19-21]. To explore the application, it is important to look for a simple, fast and environmentally friendly route to synthesize CQDs/Ag NPs composites.

In this paper, we synthesized CQDs/Ag NPs composites in-situ without additional reductants similar to the previous report [22]. CQDs were obtained by using glucose as the precursor. As a green and environmentally friendly raw material, the glucose is cheap and easy to obtain. The surface groups of as-prepared CQDs could reduce silver ions under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation. Compared with the method of adding extra reductant, utilizing UV light and CQDs to reduce silver ions is very facile and it is an environmentally friendly new technology. Interestingly, as-prepared CQDs/Ag NPs composites have better optical and antibacterial properties than sole Ag NPs or CQDs.

Glucose was purchased from Kemio (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Silver nitrate particles were obtained from North China Petrochemical Co., Ltd. Escherichia coli (CGMCC 1.2463) and Staphylococcus aureus (CGMCC 1.2910) were provided from Shanghai Luwei Technology Co., Ltd. Nutrient AGAR was purchased from Aoboxing (Beijing) Biotechnology Co., Ltd.Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs via hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared by using our previously reported hydrothermal method [23]. Briefly, 0.6 g of glucose was dissolved in 90 ml of deionized water and the solution was transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then, the system was heated at 180 °C for 24 h. Finally, the obtained CQDs were collected through the centrifugation and dialysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Ag NPs under UV light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: Solo Ag NPs samples were prepared under UV light irradiation. 5 mg of silver nitrate was dissolved in 20 ml deionized water. Then, the solution was stirred by a magnetic stirrer for 20 min and exposed to an UV lamp for 30 min, 3 h respectively. As-prepared Ag NPs samples were denoted as Ag NPs-1, Ag NPs-2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs/Ag NPs composites via in-situ method
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs/Ag NPs composites were synthesized via in-situ method assisted by UV light irradiation. 5 mg of silver nitrate, 10 ml of CQDs solution and 10 ml of deionized water were mixed by a magnetic stirrer for 20 min and then exposed to an UV lamp. The power was 200 W, and the irradiation time was 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, respectively. As-prepared CQDs/Ag NPs composite samples were denoted as CQDs/Ag NPs-1, CQDs/Ag NPs-2, CQDs/Ag NPs-3, CQDs/Ag NPs-4, CQDs/Ag NPs-5, CQDs/Ag NPs-6, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL-2010, Japan) was performed to investigate the microstructure and morphology of CQDs and CQDs/Ag NPs composites. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200i) was performed to perform element mapping characterization for investigate the component of CQDs/Ag NPs composites. TEM specimens were prepared by placing one drop of the CQDs solution and CQDs/Ag NPs composites solution on a copper grid, respectively, and were dried under an ambient condition. Surface groups of CQDs were characterized by Fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR, SHIMADZU 8400S, Japan) using potassium bromide as the matrix in the range of 500–4000 cm−1. Chemical compositions of CQDs/Ag NPs surface were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXISULTPA, England) with Al Kα X-ray source. Optical properties of samples were investigated by UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV-1800, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, F-4600, Japan).

Antibacterial properties of CQDs/Ag NPs against Gram negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (S. aureus) bacteria were confirmed by using disc diffusion method [24,25]. 16.5 g of AGAR was dissolved in 500 ml of deionized water, then, the mixture was heated and dissolved with magnetic stirrer at 100 °C. The bacterial suspension (in the range of 106-108 CFU/ml) was coated on the surface of solidified agar plate and then the filter paper with a diameter of 6 mm containing samples was placed on it. Finally, as-prepared petri dishes were placed in a constant temperature and humidity box (the temperature of 37 °C and the humidity of 70%) for 24 h to observe the growth of bacteria around filter papers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Components and morphologies of CQDs and CQDs/Ag NPs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical compositions of CQDs/Ag NPs were determined by XPS. These results are shown in Fig. 1 . The full-range XPS spectrum of CQDs/Ag NPs showed clear signals of silver, carbon, and oxygen elements (depicted in Fig. 1(a)). For the XPS spectrum of Ag 3d (depicted in Fig. 1(b)), the peaks at around 368. 6 eV and 374.6 eV were assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively, which are closed to that for Ag0 [26]. This result also confirmed that Ag+ was successful reduced to Ag0 by CQDs under the irradiation of UV light.

FTIR spectrum of CQDs is shown in Fig. 2 (a). The wide peak around 3450 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of –OH, and the peak at 1650 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of C = C [27]. According to Fig. 1(a), there were a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs, indicating that they have a very strong hydrophilicity. Furthermore, these hydroxyl groups possessed the reducibility, providing the possibility that CQDs can be used as reductants to reduce silver ions.

TEM images of CQDs and CQDs/Ag NPs are shown in Fig. 2(b)-(f). In these images, the small and light color particles were CQDs, while the large and dark color particles were Ag NPs. To further illustrate the composition of composite particles, we performed mapping characterization of CQDs/Ag NPs-6. Oppositely, the light color particles in Fig. 2(g-1) were Ag NPs due to the adjustment of background for different equipment. In addition, the red and green particles represented the Ag element and carbon element, respectively (shown in Fig. 2(g-2)-(g-4)). The content of carbon element was obviously higher than the content of Ag element (depicted in Fig. S2), which may be related to the carbon film on the copper mesh during the element mapping characterization. In addition, according to the statistical calculation on the basis of 200 particles (depicted in Fig. 2(b)), we obtained a particle size distribution of CQDs by using Nano Measurer 1.2 (depicted in Fig. S1(a)).CQDs had a uniform diameter of about 3.9 nm. According to TEM images of CQDs/Ag NPs (depicted in Fig. 2(c)-(f)), we found that most Ag NPs were formed around CQDs. The formation mechanism of CQDs/Ag NPs would be specified in section 3.2. Furthermore, some CQDs were attached to Ag NPs, and the particle size of CQDs/Ag NPs was uneven. The particle size of Ag NPs was obviously larger than that of CQDs, and the particles for CQDs/Ag NPs-1 dispersed well in the solution. With the increasing of irradiation time, the particles began to aggregate (depicted in Fig. 1(d)). Particularly, most particles for CQDs/Ag NPs-6 aggregated (depicted in Fig. 1(f)). According to the statistical calculation on the basis of 80 particles, we obtained a particle size distribution of CQDs/Ag NPs by using Nano Measurer 1.2 (depicted in Fig. S1(b-c)). The average diameter of CQDs/Ag NPs increased from about 20 nm (CQDs/Ag NPs-1) to about 28 nm (CQDs/Ag NPs-2). The possible reason is that the nucleation process of Ag NPs completed with the increasing of reaction time and these particles started to grow, leading to the increase of the particle size for as-prepared CQDs/Ag NPs. However, as the reaction time increased further, the Ostwald maturation reaction for this system was predominant [28], and the larger particles gradually merged with the smaller particles, leading to the agglomeration. Among them, the particles for CQDs/Ag NPs-3 and CQDs/Ag NPs-6 severely agglomerated. Therefore, it is difficult to accurately measure the diameter according to the dispersed particles.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the color of the solution containing CQDs and silver nitrate changed from slight yellow to dark brown and then returned to slight yellow with the increasing of UV irradiation time, confirming that silver ions have been reduced to Ag NPs by CQDs. The color change of the solution was due to the finish of the reduction of silver ions after about 30 min. Then, Ostwald Ripening reaction for this system was predominant [28], and formed CQDs/Ag NPs with smaller size gradually merged. This result was consistent with TEM images in Fig. 2(c)-(f).

The process illustration for the reduction of silver ions to Ag NPs by CQDs is shown in Fig. 3(b), mainly including photoexcitation process, nucleation process and growth process. Photoexcitation process was initial stage of the reduction. These CQDs absorbed UV light, and electrons were excited from the valence band to the conduction band, thus producing hole-electron pairs. Some groups on the surface of CQDs accumulated electrons, prompting the accumulation of silver atoms nearby CQDs [29]. Secondly, the clustered silver atoms nucleated around CQDs as the number of silver atoms increased. Thirdly, the nuclei continued to grow to form Ag NPs. When silver atoms nucleated on the surface of the CQDs, an internal electric field was generated between them, which made the band bending and accelerated the separation of electronic-hole on CQDs [30]. The electrons were transferred from CQDs to silver nuclei, and silver ions were continued to be reduced by these more electrons, accelerating the reduction process. Therefore, the rapid growth of silver nuclei led to the formation of CQDs/Ag NPs.

As shown in Fig. 4 (a), when the irradiation time was 10 min and 20 min, a peak appeared at 282 nm due to the n-π* transition of electrons under the energy radiation [31,32], representing the characteristic absorption peak of CQDs. This absorption peak disappeared with the increasing of irradiation time, indicating that there were no isolated CQDs. Then, a wide absorption band with a maximum at 442.5 nm appeared for CQDs/Ag NPs-3, which is closed to typical surface Plasmon absorption of Ag NPs [33]. When the irradiation time was 3 h, the absorption peak showed a red shift and shifted to 450 nm, which is agreement with the process of Ostwald Ripening [28]. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the absorbance for CQDs/Ag NPs-6 in the range of 325–500 nm was significantly higher than that of Ag NPs-2. These results indicated that the concentration of Ag NPs in the sample of Ag NPs-2 may be lower than CQDs/Ag NPs-6 and the addition of CQDs was helpful to the formation of Ag NPs.

Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs and CQDs/Ag NPs. The maximum emission wavelength of CQDs was 425 nm at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm (depicted in Fig. 5(a)). On the other hand, the fluorescence emission peak for CQDs/Ag NPs appeared at about 393 nm, and the fluorescence emission peak at 425 nm disappeared. As the photoluminescence was attributed to radiative combination of electrons and holes confined on the surfaces of CQDs, this result implied that the surface states of CQDs were affected by in-situ growth of Ag NPs. The fluorescence quenching was concerned to the combination of fluorescent materials with metals [34,35].

According to the mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), the resonance between CQDs and Ag NPs led to energy transfer, resulting in fluorescence quenching. This phenomenon will emerge when the emission spectrum of the donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the recipient and the distance between the two fluorescent groups is appropriate. The donor in the excited state transfers its energy to the recipient, causing the recipient to be excited. Similarly, the energy was transferred from CQDs to Ag NPs in this work [36,37]. To our knowledge, noble metals are able to capture electrons easily and these electrons can move from valence band of CQDs to the energy level of Ag NPs. In this case, the charge transfer from CQDs to Ag NPs can effectively inhibit the return of electrons to CQDs and lead to the effective fluorescence quenching of CQDs. Besides, synergistic effect of Ag NPs and CQDs in the contact interface can facilitate high-efficiency photoelectrons generation [38]. This result is consistent with the fluorescence emission peak at 393 nm for CQDs/Ag NPs.

Before conducting the formal antibacterial experiment, we first completed the pre-experiment of four samples (namely CQDs/Ag NPs-1, CQDs/Ag NPs-2, CQDs/Ag NPs-3 and CQDs/Ag NPs-6). The initial concentration of E. coli and S. aureus was diluted 10times and 102 times,respectively. These results of this pre-experiment were listed in Fig. S3 and Table S1. The four samples exhibited antibacterial properties, and that of CQDs/Ag NPs-3 and CQDs/Ag NPs-6 were better than the other two. In addition, it was preferred when the bacteria were diluted 102 times. According to the result of pre-experiment, we selected CQDs/Ag NPs-3 and CQDs/Ag NPs-6 as the preferred samples to investigate the antibacterial properties compared with CQDs, AgNO3, Ag NPs-1, Ag NPs-2. The formal antibacterial experiment would be conducted under the condition that the bacteria were diluted 102 times.

Fig. 6 shows representative images of agar plates containing sample 1 (CQDs), sample 2 (silver nitrate solution), sample 3 (as-prepared Ag NPs-1 in section 2.3), sample 4 (as-prepared Ag NPs-2 in section 2.3), sample 5 (CQDs/Ag NPs-3) and sample 6 (CQDs/Ag NPs-6) impregnated disks. To ensure the same concentration of silver in each sample, the sample 2 was obtained by dissolving 5 mg silver nitrate solid in 20 ml deionized water, then sample 2 was irradiated under UV light for 30 min and 3 h respectively to obtain sample 3 and sample 4. Sample 5 and 6 were prepared by using a mixture of sample 2 and CQDs under UV radiation for 30 min and 3 h, respectively. Thus, the concentration of silver in sample 2–6 was 2.5 mg/mL.

Antibacterial properties of these samples were estimated by the size of the inhibition zones around the sample in the Petri dish [39]. We observed that there were no obvious bacteriostatic circles around sample 1 (CQDs), while other five samples had inhibition zones. This result indicates that antibacterial activity of CQDs was poor and other five samples had obvious antibacterial effects on S. aureus and E. coli. By measuring the size of inhibition zones, the mean diameters are listed in Table 1 . Among silver nitrate solution, Ag NPs and CQDs/Ag NPs, antibacterial properties of the latter were the best. Moreover, antibacterial properties of sample 5 (CQDs/Ag NPs-3) were slightly better than sample 5 (CQDs/Ag NPs-6). The possible reason is that as-prepared sole CQDs may show antibacterial properties after they had special properties (such as hydrophobicity and photosensitivity) [40,41]. Importantly, sole Ag NPs have fairly good antibacterial properties without any modification.

On the other hand, after silver nitrate released silver ions as the antimicrobial agent, its diffusion may be blocked by the formation of secondary compounds (such as silver chloride) [39]. However, Ag NPs can be freely diffused into the culture medium and act as fungicides. It is well known that nanoparticles with very small size are more likely to penetrate the cell membrane and form membrane perforation, causing further damage by possibly interacting with sulfur-and phosphorus-containing compounds such as DNA [42]. Moreover, it is more likely to produce reactive oxygen species at the surface of these nanoparticles [43]. These factors will cause the damage to the cells. Thus, the antibacterial ability of Ag NPs was better than that of silver nitrate.

In previous reports, CQDs have been used to synthesize the composites containing metal NPs and can improve the stability of Ag NPs [44-47]. As a consequence, CQDs as the stabilizers also can improve the stability of Ag NPs for as-prepared CQDs/Ag NPs composites in this work and thus improve their antibacterial properties. Furthermore, the antibacterial property of sample 5 (CQDs/Ag NPs-3) was slightly better than that of sample 6 (CQDs/Ag NP-6) due to the smaller size of the former. Interestingly, the antibacterial activity of CQDs/Ag NPs composites against S. aureus was higher than that for E. coli. According to the antibacterial mechanism, Ag NPs can interact with the sulfhydryl and amino groups of DNA molecules. Therefore, bacterial cells cannot divide and proliferate, and eventually died [48,49]. Due to gram-positive S. aureus with loose cell walls, it is easy to be infected and vulnerable to be attacked by nanoparticles [39,48,49]. Therefore, these released Ag NPs were more likely to pass through the S. aureus cell wall and destroyed the bacteria more easily than E. coli [50].

In this paper, CQDs/Ag NPs composites have been successfully synthesized via in-situ reduction using CQDs as the reductant and stabilizer. The diameter of as-prepared CQDs/Ag NPs composites was in the range of 20–28 nm. The fluorescence emission peak for CQDs/Ag NPs appeared at about 393 nm, and that for CQDs at 425 nm disappeared due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Compared with sole Ag NPs, CQDs/Ag NPs-3 and CQDs/Ag NPs-6 showed a high antibacterial activity against both S. aureus and E. coli, and the former was preferred. CQDs as the stabilizers can improve the stability of Ag NPs and thus improve their antibacterial properties. Therefore, the optimized sample of CQDs/Ag NPs-3 in this work can be used as the potential candidate materials for antibacterial packaging.

Youliang Cheng: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Ying Wei: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Changqing Fang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jing Chen: Supervision. Weina Zhao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51772243); Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 2020GXLH-Z-031); Xi'an Science and Technology Project (Grant No. 2020KJRC0074); Key Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 20JS106).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2021.109059.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Feasibility study of preparation of carbon quantum dots from Pennsylvania anthracite and Kentucky bituminous coals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Saikia, J. C. Hower, T. Das, et al. Feasibility study of preparation of carbon quantum dots from Pennsylvania anthracite and Kentucky bituminous coals. Fuel. 2019, 243, 433-440.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, R. Ray, Y. Gu, et al. Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments. American Chemical Society. 2004, 126(40), 12736-12737.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Y. Lim, W. Shen, Z. Gao. Carbon quantum dots and their applications. Chemical Society Reviews. 2015, 44(1), 362–381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning of photoluminescence on different surface functionalized carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chandra, S. H. Pathan, S. Mitra, et al. Tuning of photoluminescence on different surface functionalized carbon quantum dots. RSC Advances. 2012, 2(9), 3602.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, properties and biomedical applications of carbon-based quantum dots: an updated review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Namdari, B. Negahdari, A. Eatemadi. Synthesis, properties and biomedical applications of carbon-based quantum dots: An updated review. Biomedicine & Pharmacotherapy. 2017, 87, 209-222.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, L. Cao, F. Lu, et al. Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots. Chemical Communications. 2009, 46(25), 3774.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The advanced role of carbon quantum dots in nanomedical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Devi, S. Saini, K. H. Kim. The advanced role of carbon quantum dots in nanomedical applications. Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 2019,141, 111158.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability under visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, H. Huang, H. Ming, et al. Carbon quantum dots/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability under visible light. Journal of Materials Chemistry. 2012, 22(21), 10501-10506.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photovoltaic conversion enhancement of a carbon quantum dots/p-type CuAlO2/n-type ZnO photoelectric device

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Pan, Y. Sheng, J. Zhang, et al. Photovoltaic conversion enhancement of a carbon quantum dots/p-type CuAlO2/n-type ZnO photoelectric device. ACS Applied Materials and Interfaces. 2015, 7(15), 7878-7883.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe2O3/carbon quantum dots complex photocatalysts and their enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, H. Ming, S. Lian, et al. Fe2O3/carbon quantum dots complex photocatalysts and their enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light. Dalton Transactions. 2011, 40(41), 10822-10825.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ZnO/carbon quantum dots nanocomposites: one-step fabrication and superior photocatalytic ability for toxic gas degradation under visible light at room temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yu, H. Zhang, H. Huang, et al. ZnO/carbon quantum dots nanocomposites: one-step fabrication and superior photocatalytic ability for toxic gas degradation under visible light at room temperature. New Journal of Chemistry. 2012, 36(4), 1031-1035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots enhance the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4 with different exposed facets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Tang, H. Zhang, H. Huang, et al. Carbon quantum dots enhance the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4 with different exposed facets. Dalton Transactions. 2013, 42(18), 6285-6289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots/Cu2O composites with protruding nano-structures and their highly efficient (near) infrared photocatalytic behavior
